advantages: Provides a principled, non-negative measure of how much a model distribution Q deviates from a reference P when data are drawn from P.
Decomposes cross-entropy cleanly: H(P, Q) = H(P) + D_KL(P || Q), separating fixed label entropy from model error.
Connects maximum likelihood, cross-entropy training, variational bounds, and policy regularization under one information-theoretic quantity.
Different argument orders (forward vs reverse KL) encode distinct approximation behaviors useful in inference and generative modeling.
disadvantages: Asymmetric and not a metric — D_KL(P || Q) ≠ D_KL(Q || P) and KL does not satisfy the triangle inequality, so it is not a distance in the geometric sense.
Infinite when supports mismatch: if q(x)=0 where p(x)>0, D_KL(P || Q)=∞, which breaks naive optimization without care.
Hard to estimate reliably from sparse samples in high dimensions; Monte Carlo KL estimates can have high variance.
Not directly interpretable in raw nats/bits to non-experts without worked examples or calibration to familiar distributions.
